note: Sharp, fine mask + visible scanlines + clean saturated colour — reads as a pro broadcast aperture-grille monitor. Verify the mask is rendered as vertical-stripe aperture grille (PVM/BVM are Trinitron-based) rather than a shadow-mask dot grid, per display-crt-mask-macro.

Sharp and clean as a late Trinitron, but the mask reads as a dot/triad grid rather than Trinitron's DEFINING aperture-grille continuous vertical RGB stripes + 1–2 faint horizontal damper-wire shadows (per display-crt-mask-macro). Mask geometry should switch to aperture grille for Trinitron-named presets.
Correct amber monochrome phosphor with a visible shadow-mask dot grid and scanlines — an authentic amber data terminal. Mask type (dot grid) matches the 'Shadow Mask' name.
Strong scanlines + slot/shadow mask + highlight bloom + saturated colour read as an arcade CRT. Plausible; could specify slot-mask geometry to distinguish from a TV shadow mask.
Fine shadow-mask dot pitch with minimal scanline modulation — correctly differentiated from an interlaced TV (a progressive VGA monitor shows dot pitch, not strong scanlines). Good.
Tasteful warm cast + glow, but same finding as WEGA: mask renders as a dot grid rather than Trinitron aperture-grille vertical stripes (display-crt-mask-macro).
Correctly soft and low-detail with horizontal scanlines and mild convergence softness — distinct from a sharp direct-view CRT, which is right for a 2004 RPTV. Good.

Correctly avoids CRT scanlines (a flat-panel must not show a raster). To sell it as plasma specifically, add the characteristic cell/pixel structure, dither/false-contour noise in gradients, and slight phosphor afterglow.

Reads as a heavy mask grid but shows NO actual burn-in: the defining trait is a faint PERSISTENT retained ghost image (station logo / static UI) etched into the phosphor, independent of the live picture. Add a retained ghost layer. Also the name 'CRT Plasma' conflates two display types with different burn-in mechanisms.

Fine RGB vertical-stripe LCD subpixel + slight grid — reads as a blocky retro LCD. Subpixel geometry judged against display-crt-mask-macro as a panel-subpixel proxy (an LCD/OLED macro would tighten this).
Clean, accurate colour, subtle subpixel — correct for IPS. Could add a faint off-angle IPS glow in the blacks. Panel-subpixel proxy reference (see display-crt-mask-macro note).

Perfect blacks + strong saturated bloom on bright elements — genuine OLED character pushed to a stylised 'cyberpunk' intensity. Intentional look; physically consistent with OLED (per-pixel emissive, no backlight bleed).

Clean and saturated, but PenTile's DEFINING diamond (RGBG, fewer red/blue) subpixel layout and the resulting colour fringing on high-contrast text/edges are not evident — reads as a generic clean panel. Add the diamond subpixel + edge fringing.

Sunlight washout (low contrast, elevated blacks, glare) is well represented and convincing. Subpixel reads as vertical stripe rather than the PenTile diamond — same subpixel gap as the sibling PenTile preset.

Bloom + fine grid present, but the look depends on a COARSE LED pixel pitch (large discrete RGB modules) plus phone-capture moiré beating against that grid — both could be pushed coarser/stronger to sell 'phone filming a billboard'.

Across the Trinitron-named presets (WEGA, Trinitron Warm Glow, PVM/BVM) the mask renders as a dot/triad grid rather than aperture-grille vertical stripes. Per display-crt-mask-macro the two geometries are distinct (shadow/slotted = dot/slot grid; Trinitron/Diamondtron = continuous vertical RGB stripes + damper-wire shadows). The mask type should switch to match the CRT each preset claims.
